note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence boundaries into caption-sized pieces. Caption text is never rewritten, summarized, or reordered. Block windows are the measured onsets of each rendered block inside the joined master (normalized cross-correlation of each WC-02-05-sNN.mp3 against WC-02-05-full-narration-v1.0.mp3); the theoretical audio+0.4 tail model runs ~0.68s long against the ffprobe master duration (301.009s) because the joined master uses ~0.36s inter-block gaps, so measured onsets are used for accurate phrase placement.

phrases.text: Fleet and corporate customers wash now and pay later.
This is where they live.
Portfolio totals up top, three insight panels, and the account list below.
When a fleet manager calls about a bill, you start on this screen.
Four numbers, the whole book.
How many accounts you carry, how many are live, what they owe you right now,
and the average credit line you extend.
Outstanding is the one your callers care about.
It is the money on the street.
Three panels read the portfolio's health.
A balance trend over the year, credit use broken out by location,
and the split of active, suspended, and closed.
Watch the utilization bars.
An account near its limit is an account about to decline.
Down to the list card.
Three buttons sit here.
One of them, Generate Invoices, runs month-end billing across every account
and sends real invoices.
We locate it and we leave it alone.
Know where it lives; never press it on a whim.
The rest is a familiar list.
Search by account, company, or contact.
Filters beside it.
Seven columns, ending in status.
If you have worked the Customer List, you already know this one.
Open one account.
Click a row and the full account view opens.
In the demo tenant every row opens the same sample account,
so read the numbers as a layout, not as one real customer.
The shape is what matters.
The header is the account at a glance.
Company name, an active badge,
then the account number, home location, and primary contact on one line.
Three chips carry the terms:
net thirty, a ten thousand dollar limit, fourteen vehicles.
You know the deal before you read a single tab.
Five buttons up top.
Back and search are safe.
Statement, delete, and edit change or generate records,
so on a support call you read them and stop.
Nothing here gets clicked today.
Six metrics run under the header.
Total balance, available credit, the limit, a monthly average,
the last payment date, and when the account opened.
Balance and available credit add up to the limit,
so one look tells you how much room is left.
Beside the band, a set of quick counts.
Vehicles, invoices, payments, documents.
They are shortcuts into the same records the tabs hold,
with the totals right on the chip.
Ten tabs across the account.
Overview, details, contacts, vehicles, invoices, payments,
activity, documents, notes, and audit trail.
You will not touch all ten on a call.
Knowing they exist means you never have to guess where a fact is stored.
Overview opens on the billing story.
Monthly charges show the average month, the peak month, and last month, over the past year.
This is the account's normal rhythm.
It is what you compare against when a charge looks wrong.
Now the panel that earns this lesson.
Aging splits what the account owes by how long it has gone unpaid:
current, thirty to sixty, sixty to ninety, ninety plus, and a total.
Older buckets are older debt.
Anything sitting in ninety plus is the account most likely to get suspended.
Read this before you promise a customer anything.
Two more panels finish the picture.
Wash frequency shows how hard the fleet is using its vehicles.
Account health rolls up credit use, a payment score, and average days to pay.
Payment score and days to pay are your fast read
on whether this account pays like clockwork or drags its feet.
Click into invoices.
Every bill the account has been sent, with its amount, what has been paid,
the balance, and a status of paid, open, or overdue.
When someone asks about a specific charge,
this is where you find the invoice behind it.
Then payments.
The ledger shows each payment, how it was applied to invoices,
the method, and who posted it.
Watch the status column.
A voided row explains a payment that looked received but was reversed,
which is often the real answer to a billing dispute.
The rest of the tabs, in a sentence each.
Details is the full record.
Contacts, the people.
Vehicles, the fleet roster.
Documents holds agreements.
Notes carries internal comments, some for everyone and some for management only.
Audit trail is the change history.
Activity would be the live feed, but it did not load at capture, so we park it.
Back to the list.
That is a house account, end to end.
The list gives you the portfolio and the billing button you never run casually.
The detail gives you one account's balance, its aging,
and the invoices and payments that answer where a charge came from.
Next time a fleet manager calls, you know the path.
